Jordan Canonical Form of a 3x3 Matrix
Introduction
?

Like the case of matrices, every matrix has a Jordan canonical decomposition. Recall that a Jordan canonical decomposition of a square matrix is given by

where is a Jordan canonical form and is an invertible matrix. So, to find a Jordan canonical decomposition of a matrix means to find both

  • a Jordan canonical form and

  • an invertible matrix that satisfies the equation above.

For example, the matrix

has the Jordan canonical decomposition where

Notice that the columns of form a basis of that consists of the following chains:

  • The vectors are generalized eigenvectors corresponding to Here, only is an eigenvector, while is a generalized eigenvector of rank (that is, is mapped to under the action of ).

  • The vector is an eigenvector corresponding to

In general, for any Jordan canonical decomposition, the matrix must satisfy the properties given below.

  • The columns of associated with a Jordan block are generalized eigenvectors corresponding to the eigenvalue

  • Moreover, if the respective columns must be in the following order:

    • the first column must be an eigenvector, and

    • the second column must be a generalized eigenvector of rank that is mapped to the first column under the action of , and so on.

Watch out! The order of the columns of is important!

Now, computing we get

Among the given vectors, only is an eigenvector of since

and we can write The other vectors do not satisfy this condition.

The Case of a Single 3x3 Jordan Block
?

In this section, we will learn how to calculate a Jordan canonical decomposition of a non-diagonalizable matrix with eigenvalue of algebraic multiplicity

Consider the matrix given by

To find a Jordan canonical decomposition of we follow the algorithm given below.

Step 1: Find the eigenvalues of the matrix.

Solving the characteristic equation we get the eigenvalue of algebraic multiplicity

Step 2. Determine the corresponding Jordan canonical form.

Computing and reducing it to row echelon form using Gaussian elimination, we obtain that This means that there is only one eigenvector corresponding to this eigenvalue, and thus the Jordan canonical form of is

Step 3: Find a basis of consisting of the generalized eigenvectors corresponding to each eigenvalue.

Applying the usual method for computing eigenvectors, we get only one eigenvector

corresponding to the eigenvalue

Since the algebraic multiplicity of is yet we need to find more linearly independent generalized eigenvectors corresponding to this eigenvalue. These generalized eigenvectors make up the columns of and form the following chain:

As a result, we obtain that

  • the first column of corresponds to the eigenvector associated to the eigenvalue

  • the second column of corresponds to the vector such that and

  • the third column of corresponds to the vector such that

To find the second vector of our basis, we have to solve the equation Solving this equation gives us

Similarly, to find the third vector of our basis, we have to solve the equation This gives us

The set indeed forms a basis of since the vectors are linearly independent.

Step 4: Construct the matrix

The columns of are the vectors and where

  • the first column must be our eigenvector

  • the second column must be the vector that is mapped to under the action of and

  • the third column must be the vector that is mapped to under the action of

So, we get

Finally, the Jordan canonical decomposition of is as follows:

Watch out! As always in Jordan decomposition, the order in which we write our vectors into the matrix is important!

The reduced matrix above has two pivot columns (the st and nd). Thus, is a free variable. From the second equation, we have Substituting this into the first equation, we obtain

The Case of Two Jordan Blocks Corresponding to Two Different Eigenvalues
?

In this section, we will learn how to calculate a Jordan canonical decomposition of a non-diagonalizable matrix where one of its eigenvalues has algebraic multiplicity

Consider the matrix given by

To find a Jordan canonical decomposition of we follow the algorithm given below.

Step 1: Find the eigenvalues of the matrix.

Solving the characteristic equation we get

  • the eigenvalue of algebraic multiplicity and

  • the eigenvalue of algebraic multiplicity

Step 2. Determine the corresponding Jordan canonical form.

Computing and reducing it to row echelon form using Gaussian elimination, we obtain that

This means that there is only one eigenvector corresponding to this eigenvalue. Therefore, a Jordan canonical form of is

Step 3: Find a basis of consisting of the generalized eigenvectors corresponding to each eigenvalue.

Applying the usual method for computing eigenvectors, we get the following:

  • One eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue namely

  • One eigenvector corresponding to the eigenvalue namely

Since the algebraic multiplicity of is yet we need to find more linearly independent generalized eigenvector corresponding to this eigenvalue.

The generalized eigenvectors make up the columns of and form the following chains:

As a result, we obtain that

  • the first column of corresponds to the eigenvector associated to the eigenvalue

  • the second column of corresponds to the vector such that

  • the third column of corresponds to the eigenvector associated to the eigenvalue

To find the second vector of our special basis, we have to solve the equation which gives us

The set indeed forms a basis of since the vectors are linearly independent.

Step 4: Construct the matrix

The columns of are the vectors and where

  • the first column must be the eigenvector

  • the second column must be the vector that is mapped to under the action of and

  • the third column must be the eigenvector

So, we get

Finally, the Jordan canonical decomposition of is as follows:
